disease.name: Neonatal Hepatitis Syndrome
disease.note: Direct (conjugated) hyperbilirubinemia presenting in the first 3 months of life. Broad syndrome encompassing idiopathic, infectious (TORCH), metabolic, and genetic causes. Must be urgently differentiated from biliary atresia (surgical emergency requiring Kasai portoenterostomy by 60 days of life).

immediateDangerSigns.sign: acholic stools from birth with absent gallbladder on ultrasound and triangular cord sign
rapidly progressive conjugated hyperbilirubinemia with rising INR
signs of acute liver failure (hypoglycemia, coagulopathy, encephalopathy)
failure to thrive with severe malnutrition

severityAssessment.severityLabel: Urgent evaluation required to distinguish from biliary atresia. Timely diagnosis within 60 days is critical for surgical outcome.

historyFeatures.symptomId: jaundice persisting beyond 2 weeks of life (prolonged jaundice)

examFeatures.signId: failure to thrive (growth parameters below centiles)

firstLine: Fractionated bilirubin (direct/conjugated >2 mg/dL or >20% of total is abnormal)
Liver function tests (ALT, AST, ALP, GGT)
TORCH serologies (CMV, toxoplasmosis, rubella, HSV IgM)
Abdominal ultrasound with Doppler (gallbladder presence/absence, triangular cord sign, choledochal cyst, hepatic vasculature)
secondLine: HIDA scan after phenobarbital pre-treatment (5 mg/kg/day x 5 days) to discriminate hepatitis from biliary atresia
Alpha-1 antitrypsin level and phenotype (PiZZ associated with neonatal hepatitis)
Sweat chloride test (cystic fibrosis associated liver disease)
Urine and serum bile acids profile
Serum amino acids and urine organic acids (tyrosinemia, other metabolic disorders)
Percutaneous liver biopsy (giant cell transformation hallmark of neonatal hepatitis)
Genetic testing for PFIC (ATP8B1, ABCB11, ABCB4), Alagille (JAG1, NOTCH2)
investigations.goldStandard: Liver biopsy demonstrating giant cell transformation of hepatocytes with intrahepatic cholestasis and excluding biliary obstruction

emergency: Immediate referral to pediatric gastroenterology/hepatology for urgent evaluation
Correct coagulopathy with vitamin K (IV or IM) and FFP if actively bleeding or before biopsy
Control sepsis with appropriate antibiotics if suspected
inpatient: Ursodeoxycholic acid (10-20 mg/kg/day) for cholestasis to improve bile flow
Nutritional support with MCT-containing formula (MCT oil bypasses impaired bile salt absorption)
Fat-soluble vitamin supplementation (ADEK) to prevent deficiency from fat malabsorption
High-calorie feeds (120-150 kcal/kg/day) to promote growth
Vitamin E as antioxidant for hepatocellular protection
Phenobarbital (3-5 mg/kg/day) may enhance bile flow and bile acid excretion
Disease-specific treatment: Valganciclovir for CMV, lactose-free formula for galactosemia, nitisinone (NTBC) for tyrosinemia type 1
outpatient: Ongoing nutritional support with MCT formula and fat-soluble vitamins
Regular monitoring of growth parameters, LFTs, GGT, and vitamin levels
Immunizations per schedule (live vaccines may be contraindicated if on immunosuppression)
Multidisciplinary follow-up (hepatology, nutrition, genetics, audiology if TORCH)
refractory: Liver transplant evaluation for progressive liver failure, refractory cholestasis, intractable pruritus, or cirrhosis
Consider repeating liver biopsy if clinical deterioration
Evaluate for alternate diagnoses if not responding to therapy
preventive: Maternal screening for TORCH infections during pregnancy
Newborn screening for galactosemia, tyrosinemia, and other metabolic disorders
Early recognition of prolonged jaundice (beyond 2 weeks) to trigger evaluation
Routine vitamin K prophylaxis at birth

disease.pathophysiology: Heterogeneous causes lead to hepatocellular injury in the neonatal period resulting in impaired bile secretion and flow. The liver responds with a nonspecific histologic pattern of giant cell transformation (fusion of hepatocytes). This produces intrahepatic cholestasis with conjugated hyperbilirubinemia, which subsequently causes malabsorption of fats and fat-soluble vitamins (A, D, E, K) leading to failure to thrive and nutritional deficiencies. The specific etiology determines the natural history, ranging from spontaneous resolution (idiopathic) to progressive liver failure (metabolic, genetic).
complications: Fat-soluble vitamin deficiency (vitamin K: coagulopathy/hemorrhage, vitamin D: rickets, vitamin A: xerophthalmia, vitamin E: neuropathy)
Intractable pruritus significantly impairing quality of life
mustNotMiss: Biliary atresia must be excluded urgently before Kasai portoenterostomy window closes (60 days of life)
Galactosemia (dietary emergency - remove galactose immediately to prevent liver failure and sepsis)
Tyrosinemia type 1 (treatable with nitisinone/NTBC to prevent liver failure and HCC)
Congenital CMV (treatable with valganciclovir to improve hearing and neurodevelopmental outcomes)
Incorrectly diagnosing physiological jaundice in a baby with conjugated hyperbilirubinemia

diagnosticClues.clue: Direct/conjugated hyperbilirubinemia in a neonate with hepatomegaly
HIDA scan shows radiotracer excretion into bowel (excludes biliary atresia)
Liver biopsy demonstrates giant cell transformation of hepatocytes
Intermittently pigmented stools (acholic stools can be transient in hepatitis vs persistently acholic in biliary atresia)
Low GGT with conjugated hyperbilirubinemia suggests PFIC1 or PFIC2

exclusionClues.clue: Acholic stools from birth with absent gallbladder on ultrasound, triangular cord sign, and no tracer on HIDA scan = biliary atresia (surgical emergency)
Dubin-Johnson and Rotor syndromes (benign conjugated hyperbilirubinemia without hepatomegaly, normal LFTs, no cholestasis)
Physiological jaundice (unconjugated, peaks day 3-5, no hepatomegaly, resolves spontaneously)
Choledochal cyst on ultrasound (surgically correctable extrahepatic obstruction)
Alagille syndrome (facial features, cardiac murmur, butterfly vertebrae, posterior embryotoxon, paucity of bile ducts on biopsy)
